textSnippets: We demonstrated that HCW9206, a unique protein scaffold linking interleukin-7 (IL-7), an IL-15/IL-15 receptor α (IL-15Rα) complex, and IL-21, generates CAR T cells without requiring αCD3/28 activation, which are highly enriched in long-lived T memory stem cells (TSCM cells) (>50%) and display potent activity across distinct disease models, HIV-1 or B cell leukemia.
In a humanized mouse HIV infection model, HCW9206-generated anti-HIV duoCAR T cells suppressed viremia more effectively than αCD3/28-generated anti-HIV duoCAR T cells.

In a xenograft leukemia mouse model, a recall proliferative response and complete clearance of leukemia rechallenge were displayed by HCW9206-generated but not by αCD3/28-generated antiCD19 CAR T cells.

HCW9206, a first-in-class cytokine scaffold–based platform, enables production of more potent CAR T cell–based immunotherapies by generating a CAR T cell population, which is highly functional and also markedly enriched for long-lived TSCM cells.

Together, these data demonstrate that treating T cells with a combination of IL-7, IL-15SA, and IL-21 cytokines delivered by a single biologic, HCW9206, can quickly and effectively produce HIVand CD19-specific CAR T cells that are highly enriched for the TSCM cell memory phenotype, as well as effector T cells capable of maintaining suppression of HIV and leukemic cell proliferation.

Therefore, generating CAR T cells through HCW9206 treatment could provide a new, improved, and highly scalable method for generating CAR T cells to treat patients with cancer and infectious diseases and replace standard CAR T cell production using αCD3/28 activation.
This has widespread implications for the generation of more robust CAR T cell–based immunotherapies to improve CAR T cell functional persistence and efficacy for the treatment of HIV and cancer.

pages.text: Sci Adv. 2026 Mar 13;12(11):eaec2632. doi: 10.1126/sciadv.aec2632 IL-7/IL-15/IL-21 cytokine-fusion scaffold generates highly functional CAR T cells enriched in long-lived T memory stem cells Erin B Cole 1, Sara Lamcaj 1, Agnes V Sydenstricker 1, Adilyn G Voss 1, Christopher R Hiner 1, Hong B Hur 2, Manoj Kandpal 2, Natalia Valderrama Pena 3, Jian Hua Zheng 1, Ying Xiong 4, Zhongyu Zhu 4, Cheng Cheng Zhang 5, Niraj Shrestha 3, Boro Dropulic 3, Hing C Wong 3, Harris Goldstein 1,6,* Author information Article notes Copyright and License information PMCID: PMC12985740 PMID: 41824575 Abstract Functional persistence of chimeric antigen receptor T cells (CAR T cells) is limited by conventional CAR T cell manufacturing using anti-CD3/CD28 (αCD3/28) stimulation, which generates terminally differentiated and shorter-lived CAR T cells. We demonstrated that HCW9206, a unique protein scaffold linking interleukin-7 (IL-7), an IL-15/IL-15 receptor α (IL-15Rα) complex, and IL-21, generates CAR T cells without requiring αCD3/28 activation, which are highly enriched in long-lived T memory stem cells (TSCM cells) (>50%) and display potent activity across distinct disease models, HIV-1 or B cell leukemia. In a humanized mouse HIV infection model, HCW9206-generated anti-HIV duoCAR T cells suppressed viremia more effectively than αCD3/28-generated anti-HIV duoCAR T cells. In a xenograft leukemia mouse model, a recall proliferative response and complete clearance of leukemia rechallenge were displayed by HCW9206-generated but not by αCD3/28-generated antiCD19 CAR T cells. HCW9206, a first-in-class cytokine scaffold–based platform, enables production of more potent CAR T cell–based immunotherapies by generating a CAR T cell population, which is highly functional and also markedly enriched for long-lived TSCM cells. This strategy is broadly

applicable to increase persistence and functionality of CAR T cells, enhancing their efficacy for treating infectious disease and cancer. Scaffold-linked IL-7/IL-15/IL-21 treatment generates highly functional CAR T cells substantially enriched for T memory stem cells. INTRODUCTION Chimeric antigen receptor T cell (CAR T cell) therapy has revolutionized the cancer immunotherapy field with Food and Drug Administration approval of five CAR T cell therapeutics for the treatment of leukemia, lymphoma, and, more recently, multiple myeloma (1–8). CAR T cell therapy has been extended beyond cancer therapy into the treatment of chronic infectious diseases, particularly as a treatment to provide a functional cure for HIV-1 in people living with HIV (PLWH). However, the effectiveness of CAR T cells to persistently suppress HIV faces many challenges, including the high mutation rate of reverse transcriptase, which enables the emergence of immune escape viral variants in parallel with recurrent viremia after the cessation of antiretroviral therapy (ART) from a population of latently infected cells (9–12). To circumvent immune escape by HIV, these HIV-targeting CAR T cells must be engineered to recognize highly conserved epitopes in the HIV-1 envelope (Env) glycoprotein gp120 expressed on the surface of HIV-infected cells. First-generation anti–HIV-1 CAR T cells targeting the CD4 binding domain of HIV-1 gp120 with a human CD4 extracellular domain fused to a CD3ζ signaling domain only minimally controlled HIV-1 infection due to the limited T cell signaling delivered by CARs containing only the CD3ζ T cell receptor (TCR) signaling domain and the unintended consequence of the CD4-based CAR conferring CD8+ T cells with susceptibility to HIV infection (13–15). We previously reported overcoming these limitations by constructing duoCAR T cells, second-generation CAR T cells expressing two independent CAR domains targeting two highly conserved HIV-Env epitopes linked to CD3ζ and 4-1BB costimulatory signaling domains; the anti-HIV CAR molecules, mD1.22-CAR and m36.4-CAR, target the highly conserved CD4 and CCR5 co-receptor binding sites of HIV, respectively (16, 17). Although these multispecific anti-HIV duoCAR T cells display potent suppression of HIV infection in vivo, to date, none of the tested anti-HIV CAR T cells have demonstrated long-term HIV suppression following discontinuation of ART (13, 14, 18–20). We hypothesized that one factor reducing the functional persistence of CAR T cells may be the standard method used for activating T cells to enable their transduction by CAR-encoding lentivirus, which involves T cell activation through TCR signaling with antibodies to CD3 and CD28 (αCD3/28). While this method enables highly efficient lentivirus transduction, it also generates a more differentiated CAR T cell product that correlates with reduced persistence of cytotoxic function (21, 22). Previous studies of CD19 CAR T cell therapy have shown that patients with the best clinical outcomes often have CAR T cells with a less differentiated, early

memory phenotype, particularly T memory stem cells (TSCM cells), which are characterized by the expression of markers such as CD45RO−, CCR7+, CD95+, CD45RA+, CD62L+, CD27+, CD28+, and interleukin-7 receptor α+ (IL-7Rα+) (23, 24). The TSCM cell subset represents the earliest stage of T cell differentiation following naïve T cells (TN cells), and consequently, both T cell subpopulations share many functional and phenotypic characteristics, such as expressing the same T cell marker, CCR7, but not CD45RO (TN/SCM cells: CD45RO−CCR7+). TSCM cells can be distinguished from TN cells by their expression of CD95; thus, TSCM cells are characterized as CD45RO−CCR7+CD95+. This very early stage of T cell differentiation confers the ability to differentiate into diverse T memory cell progeny and exhibit self-renewal, high proliferation, and long-term survival in vivo (25, 26). This TSCM cell phenotype is crucial for generating the most effective HIV-specific CAR T cells, as the continuous presence of functional anti-HIV CAR T cells is necessary for sustained HIV suppression. This is important to prevent viral rebound from long-lived latently infected cell populations that can reintroduce dispersed HIV infection after ART is stopped (3, 17, 25). However, the generation of substantial populations of TSCM CAR T cells using standard approaches is hindered by the rarity of TSCM cell populations, which comprise only 2 to 3% of circulating T cells (25). While this can be circumvented by generating CAR T cells from highly purified TN or TSCM cells, obtaining these TSCM cells requires starting with a very large population of T cells. In turn, the generated CAR TSCM cells display reduced cytotoxic function and cytokine production (27). An alternative protocol generated substantial populations of TSCM CAR T cells by stimulating highly purified naïve CD8+ T cells with soluble αCD3 antibody alone, followed by transduction with a CARencoding lentivirus and treatment with IL-7 and IL-15 for 4 weeks of culture (28). While culturing CAR T cells with IL-7, IL-15, and IL-21 after generation by αCD3/28 stimulation was reported to increase the population of CAR T cells displaying naive and early memory phenotypes compared to culture with IL-2, this study did not specifically evaluate the generation of TSCM CAR T cells (29). Other methods have been used to prolong the persistence of CAR T cells, including blocking T cell differentiation through activation of Wnt signaling (30) and generating CAR T cells that preserve stemlike memory activity through IL-10 immunosuppressive signaling (31). In this study, we demonstrate that HCW9206, a soluble tissue-factor (TF) fusion molecule that links IL-7, an IL-15/IL- 15Rα sushi domain complex, and IL-21 to activate and expand memory-like natural killer (NK) cells (32), enables highly efficient T cell transduction with CAR-encoding lentivirus to generate both HIVand CD19-specific CAR T cells. These CAR T cells are composed of both a large population of CAR TSCM cells and sufficient CAR T effector cells to effectively suppress HIV infection or eliminate CD19+ tumor cells. The generation of CAR T cells by HCW9206 treatment represents a marked improvement over CAR T cell production by standard αCD3/28 activation, as they consist of a several-fold higher fraction of CAR TSCM cells, enabling them to display more persistent functional activity and potent effector functions. RESULTS

Treatment with HCW9206, a scaffold protein linking IL-7, an IL-15/IL-15Rα sushi domain complex, and IL-21, stimulates proliferation of CD8+ T cells particularly those with a TSCM cell phenotype To deliver optimal cytokine support for expanding antitumor memory-like NK cells ex vivo, we developed a multifunctional fusion molecule, HCW9206, which links human IL-7, an IL-15/IL-15Rα sushi domain complex (IL-15SA), which functions as an IL-15 superagonist (33), and IL-21 (Fig. 1A). We previously verified the biological activity of the HCW9206-linked IL-15SA, IL-7, and IL-21 by demonstrating their capacity to drive proliferation of the cytokine-dependent cell lines CTLL-2, 2E8, and B9, respectively (32). To further demonstrate the biological activity of the cytokines linked to HCW9206 in primary T cells, we assessed their ability to activate signal transducer and activator of transcription 5 (STAT5) and STAT3, transcription factors that are phosphorylated during the Janus kinase (JAK)/STAT signaling cascade; STAT5 phosphorylation is initiated by IL-7 and IL-15, and, to a lesser extent, IL-21 and STAT3 phosphorylation is induced by IL-7, IL-15, or IL-21 (34). After treatment of donor-derived CD8+ T cells with HCW9206 (100 nM) or with IL-7, IL-15, or IL-21 (1 nM), STAT5 phosphorylation was induced by IL-7, IL-15, and HCW9206 but not by IL-21 (Fig. 1, B and C). A total of 100 nM HCW9206 was identified as an optimal dose through a titration analysis that evaluated proliferation of CAR T cells and generation of CAR TSCM cells (fig. S1, A and B). STAT3 was phosphorylated by treatment with HCW9206, IL-7, IL-15, and IL-21 cytokines, whereas STAT5 was not significantly phosphorylated after IL-21 treatment, corresponding to its primary induction of STAT1 and STAT3 phosphorylation (Fig. 1, B and D) (34). These data indicate that HCW9206 induces phosphorylation of both STAT5 and STAT3, consistent with the activities of its linked cytokines.

We compared the capacity of HCW9206 treatment to induce proliferation of T cells to αCD3/28 stimulation with added IL-2 by treating Cell Trace Violet (CTV)–stained donor human T cells with HCW9206 or αCD3/28 for 7 days. Proliferation evaluated by flow cytometric quantification of dye dilution demonstrated that the fraction of CD8 T cells that underwent proliferation following HCW9206 treatment (86.3 ± 7.2%) was comparable to that observed with αCD3/28 treatment (97.8 ± 1.2%) (Fig. 1, E to G). However, the fraction (92.1 ± 3.8%) of CD8 T cells in the TN/SCM cell memory quadrant (CD45RO−CCR7+) that underwent proliferation after HCW9206 treatment was significantly higher (P = 0.0021) than the fraction of CD45RO−CCR7+ CD8 T cells (43.7 ± 18.5%) that proliferated in response to αCD3/28 stimulation (Fig. 1G) This likely results from the CD8 T cells that proliferate in response to αCD3/28, down-regulating CCR7, with the remaining small population in that quadrant being TN cells rather than TSCM cells, which makes them less prone to proliferation. These results indicate that HCW9206 promotes the proliferation of CD8 T cells, but unlike αCD3/28 treatment, it also preferentially induces the proliferation of TSCM-like cells, consistent with previous reports that IL-7 and IL-15, homeostatic cytokines, can drive the proliferation of TSCM cells, but not TN cells (25). The treatment of human T cells with HCW9206 enables highly efficient lentiviral transduction and generation of CAR T cells with a predominant TSCM cell phenotype

We hypothesized that we could enhance the persistence of CAR T cells by developing a cytokinebased alternative to the standard CAR T cell generation method of αCD3/28 activation to enable the generation of CAR T cells with greater proliferative and self-renewal capacity. Previous studies reported that treatment with cytokines such as IL-7 or IL-15 would enable T cells to be transduced with lentivirus to generate early memory T cells engineered to express defined TCRαβ chains or CD19-targeting CAR that display improved therapeutic activity (21, 35, 36). We examined whether treatment with a combination of IL-7, IL-15SA, and IL-21 linked by a scaffold would confer susceptibility to lentiviral transduction on T cells and enable the generation of CAR T cells with a predominant TSCM cell phenotype, as well as CAR T cells with potent effector activity. We compared the CAR T cells generated from donor-derived T cells transduced with a lentiviral vector expressing an anti-HIV duoCAR (Fig. 2A) (16). Cells were activated either with standard αCD3/28 activation in the presence of recombinant human cytokines (e.g., IL-2) or with HCW9206 treatment alone (Fig. 2B). Comparable transduction efficiency with the duoCAR lentivirus was observed for T cells after standard αCD3/28 stimulation and IL-2 treatment (duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells); αCD3/28 stimulation, followed by treatment with IL-15 or IL-7, IL-15, and IL-21; or HCW9206 (duoCAR THCW9206 cells) treatment as indicated by expression of the two duoCAR gp120 binders, m36.4 and mD1.22 (Fig. 2, C and D, and fig. S2A). However, the fraction of duoCAR T cells that were TSCM cells was ~14-fold higher in the duoCAR THCW9206 cell population (58.7 ± 8.5%) compared to the duoCAR TαCD3/28 cell population (4.1 ± 2.9%), as indicated by their expression of TSCM cell phenotype markers, CD45RO−CCR7+CD95+ (P < 0.0001) (Fig. 2E and fig. S2, B to D). We observed that TSCM cell expansion occurred with IL-15 treatment alone but not with αCD3/28 stimulation combined with IL- 15 or with a mix of IL-7, IL-15, and IL-21. This is likely a consequence of the potent αCD3/28 stimulation signal driving differentiation of TSCM cells, overriding the capacity of IL-15 alone or in combination with IL-7 and IL-21, to sustain early memory TSCM cells (37). In contrast, IL-15 alone supported TSCM cell enrichment but to a significantly lesser extent than treatment with a combination of IL-7, IL-15, and IL-21 delivered by HCW9206 treatment (P = 0.0269). We then evaluated the capacity of these TSCM CAR T cells to display the characteristic capacity of TSCM cells to generate diverse T memory cell progeny in response to antigen stimulation (25, 38). We compared the memory phenotype of duoCAR THCW9206 or duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells before and after gp120 stimulation for 4 days by coculture (2:1 effector:target ratio) with human embryonic kidney (HEK) 293T cells expressing the HIV-1 envelope protein gp120 (293T-gp120 cells). After gp120 stimulation, there was a shift of the predominant duoCAR THCW9206 cell phenotype from TSCM cells (59.8 ± 7.7%) to the more differentiated central memory (TCM) T cell phenotype (50.3 ± 3.7%) with a commensurate reduction in the TSCM cell population to 25.5 ± 3.0% (Fig. 2F).

We further evaluated the capacity of HCW9206 treatment to generate CAR T cells by investigating its ability to produce CD19-specific CAR T cells (Fig. 3A). As observed for duoCAR T cell production, while HCW9206 treatment generated levels of CD19 CAR T cells (CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells) comparable to the standard αCD3/28 method (CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells) (Fig. 3B and fig. S3A), the phenotype of CAR T cells that was TSCM cells (CD45RO−CCR7+CD95+) was ~12-fold higher compared to CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells (P < 0.0001) (Fig. 3, C and D, and fig. S3B). We also determined that at an equimolar concentration (1 nM), HCW9206 treatment led to a significant (P = 0.03), almost twofold, increase in the population of CD45RO−CCR7+CD95+ CD19 CAR TSCM cells (69.60 ± 14.9%) compared to IL-7 stimulation alone (37.58 ± 6.6%) (Fig. 3E). The TSCM cell phenotype of these CD45RO−CCR7+CD95+ CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells was further confirmed by their coexpression of several markers associated with T cell stemness, including CCR7, CD45RA, CD27, and CD95 (Fig. 3C and fig. S3C); absence of expression of the T virtual memory marker cell, NKG2A (fig. S3D); and expression of the T cell progenitor–associated transcription factor T cell factor 1 (TCF-1) (fig. S3D). As observed for the duoCAR THCW9206 cells, we observed a marked shift for the TCM cell phenotype in the CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells after antigen stimulation by CD19 delivered by a 4-day culture with CD19+ NALM-6 cells, with the average population of TSCM cells decreasing from 58.7 ± 4.0 to 4.4 ± 1.9% while the average population of TCM cells increased from 20.1 ± 2.7 to 74.2 ± 3.1% (Fig. 3F). Together, these data demonstrate that HCW9206 treatment generates CAR T cells that are markedly enriched for early memory TSCM cells.

CAR T cells generated by HCW9206 treatment or αCD3/28 activation display comparable in vitro functional activity We compared the functional capacity of duoCAR THCW9206 cells and duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells to suppress infection by an HIV-1 infectious molecular clone (IMC) expressing a Renilla luciferase reporter gene (HIV-LucR) as previously described (16, 17). Phytohemagglutinin (PHA)–activated CD4+ T cells were infected with HIV-LucR expressing either the BaL or JRCSF Env glycoprotein, cultured with the indicated syngeneic duoCAR T cells, and the level of HIV infection was determined by quantifying luciferase activity several days later. DuoCAR THCW9206 and duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells exhibited comparable in vitro suppression of HIV-1 infection in a dose-dependent manner, achieving >90% suppression at a 1:10 effector:target ratio following infection with an IMC expressing the JRCSF envelope (Fig. 4A) and >67% suppression with an IMC expressing BaL envelope (Fig. 4B). Similarly, CD19 CAR THCW9206 and CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells exhibited equivalent cytotoxicity against CD19+ tumor cell lines, including Raji cells (Fig. 4C) and NALM-6 cells (Fig. 4D).

Anti-HIV duoCAR THCW9206 cells display increased TSCM cell transcriptional programming and effector signatures compared to duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells To further evaluate the differences between anti-HIV duoCAR T cells generated by standard αCD3/28 stimulation and HCW9206 treatment, we compared their transcriptomes with or without stimulation with 293T-gp120 cells. CD8+ duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells and duoCAR THCW9206 cells generated from two donors were either cultured with 293T cells (unactivated) or cultured with 293T-gp120 cells (activated) for 16 hours, purified by flow cytometric sorting, and their transcriptomes (N = 2 biological replicates) were evaluated by bulk RNA sequencing (Fig. 5A). With or without stimulation with 293T-gp120 cells, duoCAR THCW9206 cells exhibited distinct transcriptional differences as compared to duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells (fig. S5). In the comparison of duoCAR THCW9206 and duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells with or without stimulation with gp120, we identified 311 unique differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in the absence of gp120 activation, 172 DEGs after gp120 activation, and 128 shared DEGs (Fig. 5B). These results indicate that the largest transcriptional differences between duoCAR THCW9206 and duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells are observed in unactivated CAR T cells; however, upon activation, their transcriptional profiles converge with lower amounts of DEGs, indicating an overlapping response to CAR stimulation by gp120. Specifically, we found that unactivated CAR THCW9206 cells displayed an up-regulation of cytokine signaling– and JAK/STAT signaling–associated gene transcripts as compared to unactivated CAR TαCD3/28 cells, including STAT3, IL15RA, IL-2RA, IL6R, SOCS1, SOCS2, SOCS3, FOS, and JUN (fig. S6A, fig. S7 and S8), as well as proinflammatory cytokines such as TNF and IFNG (fig. S5B).

CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells display increased in vivo functional persistence compared to CAR TαCD3/28 cells in Prkdcscid RhRHIL-2rgtm1Wjl/SzJ mice rechallenged with NALM-6 leukemia cells We examined whether enrichment of the CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells for the TSCM cell phenotype, compared to the CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells, increased their in vivo functional persistence by evaluating their ability to eliminate an initial challenge and subsequent rechallenge with NALM-6 leukemia cells expressing a luciferase reporter gene (NALM-6–Luc cells) injected into Prkdcscid RhRHIL-2rgtm1Wjl/SzJ (NSG) mice (Fig. 6A). One day after NSG mice were intravenously engrafted with NALM-6–Luc cells (0.5 × 106 cells per mouse), the mice were intravenously injected with CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells or CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells (8:1 effector:target ratio). Human IL-7, IL-15SA, and IL-21 to support the engrafted human T cells in the mice were provided by biweekly injections with HCW9206 (3 mg/kg) (43, 44). Preliminary studies indicated that CAR T cell function and persistence were enhanced in mice injected with HCW9206 compared to mice not treated with HCW9206 (fig. S10, A and B). Even in mice not treated with cytokines, we observed a trend of enhanced CD19 CAR THCW9206 survival and delayed NALM-6–Luc cell expansion compared to CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cell–treated mice (fig. S10, C and D). The population of NALM-6–Luc cells in mice was quantified weekly via bioluminescence imaging (BLI) (Fig. 6B). Rapid expansion and systemic dissemination of NALM-6–Luc cells were observed 20 days after they were injected into mice that were either untreated or treated with untransduced T cells and either activated by αCD3/28 or treated with HCW9206 before engraftment. In contrast, we observed almost total inhibition of NALM-6–Luc cell growth in all the mice treated with CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells or CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells. To evaluate the persistence of functional CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells or CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells in these mice 3 weeks after the initial CD19 CAR T cell injection, we rechallenged them with a second injection of NALM-6–Luc cells (0.5 × 106). Evaluation of the mice by BLI demonstrated that while NALM-6–Luc cell dissemination was observed in five of six mice injected with CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells, no NALM-6–Luc cell dissemination was detected in any of the five mice infused with CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells (Fig. 6B). This was confirmed by a significantly (P = 0.0321) reduced total photon flux measured in the mice treated with CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells compared to mice treated with CAR TαCD3/28 cells (Fig. 6C). The potent suppression of NALM-6–Luc cell expansion after rechallenge in the mice treated with CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells was associated with a significant increase in the number of circulating CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells at 8 days (P = 0.0053) and 16 days (P = 0.0008) compared to barely detectable levels of circulating CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells in the CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cell–treated mice (Fig. 6D). This was paralleled by the consistently higher population of CD19 CAR T cells with a TSCM cell phenotype in the peripheral blood of mice injected with CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells compared to mice injected with CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells (Fig. 6, E and F). Specifically, 15 days after infusion, the fraction of CD19 CAR T cells with a TSCM cell phenotype in the peripheral blood of mice injected with CAR THCW9206 cells (46.4 ± 25.9%) was almost 20-fold greater than the TSCM cell population in the mice injected with CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells (2.5 ± 5.6%); this likely contributes to the more

persistent in vivo functional activity of CAR THCW9206 cells compared to mice injected with CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells. Furthermore, over 6 weeks after injection of the CD19 CAR T cells and 3 weeks after rechallenging with NALM-6–Luc cells, we detected few CAR T cells in the spleens (Fig. 6G) and bone marrow (Fig. 6H) of mice injected with CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells. In contrast, we detected discrete populations of CD19 CAR T cells in the spleens (Fig. 6G) and bone marrows (Fig. 6H) of all the mice injected with CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells, which were significantly higher (P = 0.0014 and P = 0.0098, respectively) than CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cell–injected mice. Together, these data demonstrate that the CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells exhibit robust engraftment, with significantly higher populations of CAR TSCM cells. This population is associated with enhanced persistence and a greater capacity to mount a protective proliferative response in vivo, as indicated by the effective suppression of tumor cell expansion after rechallenging with CD19+ NALM-6–Luc cells by CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells compared to CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells.

Anti-HIV duoCAR THCW9206 cells display potent cytotoxic function in an HIV-1 peripheral blood mononuclear cell humanized mouse model We compared the capacity of duoCAR THCW9206 cells and duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells to suppress HIV infection using a humanized NSG mouse model of HIV-1 infection (hu-spl-PBMC-NSG) we had previously developed to establish the capacity of intravenously injected anti-HIV duoCAR T cells to migrate into the spleen to suppress HIV infection (17). As outlined in the experimental protocol (Fig. 7A), after NSG mice were intrasplenically injected with syngeneic peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) infected with HIV-1JRCSF (15 × 106 cells per mouse), they were either untreated or treated with controls of untransduced αCD3/28-activated T cells or HCW9206-treated T cells or with duoCAR THCW9206 cells or duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells (15 × 106 cells per mouse). The mice were euthanized after 17 to 18 days, and duoCAR T cells, human lymphocytes, and the level of HIV infection in the spleens were quantified. While equivalent numbers of duoCAR T cells were detected in the spleens of mice injected either with duoCAR THCW9206 cells or duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells (Fig. 7B), the spleens of mice injected with duoCAR THCW9206 cells displayed significantly greater (P = 0.0173) suppression of the number of HIV-infected cells (92.4%) compared to mice injected with duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells (49.7%) as evaluated through an unpaired nonparametric student’s t test (Fig. 7C). We also observed an increased population of human CD4+ T cells in the spleens of duoCAR THCW9206 cell–injected mice compared to duoCAR TαCD3/28 cell–injected mice (Fig. 7D), likely reflecting an inverse correlation between the level of HIV infection in the mouse spleens and HIV-mediated CD4+ T cell depletion. Together, these data indicate that duoCAR THCW9206 cells display more potent in vivo anti-HIV activity

than duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells, possibly due to the enrichment of the TSCM cell phenotype in the engrafted duoCAR THCW9206 cells, which may provide a continuing source of duoCAR TEM and effector cells that enable increased suppression of HIV infection.

Anti-HIV duoCAR THCW9206 cells with potent effector function can be generated from PLWH The goal of duoCAR T cell treatment is to provide a persistent immune response capable of mediating sustained remission to PLWH. Consequently, we evaluated whether HCW9206 treatment of PLWH T cells also generated duoCAR T cells enriched with a TSCM cell phenotype, which should display the more persistent functional activity required to provide the sustained suppression of recurrent HIV infection, which would otherwise occur after the cessation of ART. As observed for people living without HIV (PLWoH) donors, HCW9206 treatment enabled equivalent transduction efficiency with the duoCAR T lentivirus of T cells from PLWH compared to αCD3/28 activation (Fig. 8A). Furthermore, the fraction of CD8+ duoCAR T cells generated by HCW9206 treatment, which were phenotypically TSCM cells (CD45RO−CCR7+CD95+), was ~15-fold greater (48.2 ± 18.0%) than the fraction of duoCAR T cells that were TSCM cells (3.2 ± 3.7%) produced by αCD3/28 activation as demonstrated by CD45RO versus CCR7 expression in contour plots and CD95 and CD27 in histogram plots (Fig. 8, B and C). We evaluated the capacity of the duoCAR THCW9206 cells compared to the duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells from PLWH donors to suppress HIV infection using an in vitro HIV-1 suppression assay as previously described (17). Briefly, we superinfected CD8-depleted PBMCs derived from PLWH donors with the HIV-1-LucR IMC expressing the BaL envelope and 2 days later added autologous duoCAR THCW9206 cells or duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells at a 1:1 effector:target ratio. As compared to HIV-infected CD8-depleted PBMCs alone and CD8-depleted PBMCs treated with UTD control T cells, both duoCAR THCW9206 cells and duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells suppressed HIV-1 infection equivalently by >75% as shown in a representative experiment (Fig. 8D) and combined data for three PLWH donors (Fig. 8E). Thus, HCW9206 treatment of T cells from PLWH generates duoCAR T cells composed of a highly enriched TSCM cell population, which supports long-term persistence and
functional activity in vivo, along with a TEM cell population capable of providing immediate and potent HIV-1 suppression.

DISCUSSION Despite the capacity of ART to suppress HIV replication below the level of detection, sustained remission for PLWH after the cessation of ART is prevented by the persistence of the HIV viral reservoir capable of reintroducing systemic HIV (11). One strategy to achieve a functional cure for HIV is to augment the immune system of PLWH by introducing persistent, circulating immune cells with the capacity to recognize highly conserved HIV antigens (13). These cells should be resistant to HIV infection, able to overcome viral immune escape, and capable of migrating to sites of latent HIV infection to enable them to persist and quickly eliminate HIV-infected cells after reactivation to prevent recurrence of systemic infection. For this purpose, we developed and validated the potent anti-HIV activity of bispecific duoCAR T cells (16). The duoCAR T cells, which target two highly conserved epitopes of the HIV-1 envelope glycoprotein, gp120, are highly resistant to HIV infection and migrate to active infection sites to effectively suppress HIV-1 infection in vivo in a hu-spl-PBMCNSG mouse model (16, 17). However, the long-term efficacy of current CAR T cell immunotherapy is limited by the loss of their functional activity over time after infusion into patients, particularly if their maturational state predominantly consists of terminally differentiated effector cells, which upregulate the expression of exhaustion markers and are associated with poor in vivo engraftment and antitumor activity (24, 45). In contrast, T cells comprising early memory T cell phenotypes exhibit superior engraftment and better clinical outcomes after adoptive transfer compared to T cells derived from more differentiated memory precursors (46). One reason for the reduced persistence of infused CAR T cells may be the use of strong CD3 and CD28 activation to enable transduction with lentivirus encoding the CAR, which has the unintended consequence of driving rapid T cell differentiation and the production of suboptimal CAR T cell products (23, 47–50). To address this, alternative strategies that do not involve CD3 and CD28 stimulation have been evaluated, which deliver sufficient activation to enable lentiviral transduction and the generation of CAR T cells but induce a decreased level of T cell differentiation. One strategy replaced TCR activation with IL-7 treatment. IL-7 increases

the expression of the antiapoptotic molecule BCL2 and induces G0 to G1 cell-cycle progression, enabling lentiviral transduction without driving T cell differentiation (36). In the absence of CD3 activation, IL-7 treatment enabled transduction by lentivirus to create TCR-modified T cells or CD19 CAR T cells enriched in a TSCM cell phenotype, which demonstrated improved tumor control in in vivo melanoma and lymphoma xenograft models compared to αCD3/28-stimulated cells (21, 36). However, these studies could not confirm the corresponding persistence of the circulating IL-7- stimulated engineered T cells. Similarly, previous studies have also shown that IL-7 and IL-15 better preserve the TSCM cell phenotype (51, 52). One study used isolated TN cells activated with αCD3/28 and cultured with IL-7 and IL-15 to generate CD19 CAR T cells enriched in the TSCM cell subset (27). Although these CAR TSCM cells displayed enhanced tumor suppression and corresponding CAR T cell persistence, they were less cytotoxic compared to more differentiated CAR T cell subsets (27). In the current study, we build on this prior research by incorporating IL-21 alongside IL-7 and IL-15SA delivered by a single scaffold protein to support the expansion of T cells with potent effector function while maintaining early memory phenotypes, including TCM and TSCM cell subsets (51, 53, 54). Previous reports have shown that IL-21 is crucial for the development and maintenance of early memory CD8 T cells (55), and it explicitly enhances survival and proliferation when combined with IL-7 or IL-15, benefiting both memory and naïve-like CAR T cells (56). To the best of our knowledge, we are the first study to demonstrate the use of a novel tripartite cytokine scaffold for generating TSCM cell-enriched, polyfunctional CAR T cells. Notably, this study is also the first to use only cytokine-based activation signals for generating HIV-specific CAR T cells. This advances HIV immunotherapy by introducing a new strategy that can produce a functionally persistent product, thereby extending the life span of anti-HIV CAR T therapy in PLWH and potentially enabling a functional cure. To deliver IL-7, IL-15SA, and IL-21 using a single agent, we developed HCW9206, a soluble TF fusion molecule that links IL-7, IL-15SA, and IL-21 to activate and expand memory-like NK cells (32). This scaffold is based on the IL-15 superagonist, N-803, and uses the corresponding IL-15/IL-15RaSu domains (57). However, HCW9206 builds on N-803 by additionally linking IL-7 and IL-21 cytokine domains, all joined by a TF-based scaffold. This design simplifies the delivery of three synergistic cytokines in a single treatment and allows each cell interacting with the scaffold to engage all three cytokines simultaneously. Here, we demonstrate that multicytokine scaffold treatment with HCW9206, without additional CD3 activation, enables the efficient production of both HIVand CD19-specific CAR T cells that are highly enriched for a TSCM cell phenotype. HCW9206 treatment also generates sufficient CAR T effector cells that can elicit an immediate cytotoxic response, effectively suppress HIV infection, and eliminate CD19+ tumor cells. These results indicate that HCW9206 treatment provides benefits over standard αCD3/28 activation, as it generates both HIVspecific duoCAR T cells (16, 17) and CD19-specific CAR T cells enriched in CAR TSCM cells. These cells display potent in vivo suppression of HIV infection and leukemic cell growth, along with enhanced functional persistence (Figs. 6 and 7).

We demonstrated that HCW9206-generated CAR T cells, highly enriched in TSCM cells, display a shift to a more differentiated memory and effector phenotype distribution after coculture with their cognate antigen, either HIV gp120 or CD19, which indicated that the HCW9206-generated CAR T cells were capable of both proliferating and expanding more differentiated T memory cell progeny, both of which are critical for optimal effector responses (Figs. 2F and 3F). Previous studies that generated CAR TSCM cells by prepurifying TSCM cells before CAR lentiviral transduction produced CAR T cells that were less cytotoxic than conventional αCD3/28-generated CAR T cells (27). In contrast, we demonstrate, both in vitro and in vivo, that HCW9206-generated HIV-specific duoCAR T cells are markedly enriched for the TSCM cell phenotype while also exhibiting cytotoxic function equivalent to that of CAR TαCD3/28 cells (Fig. 4). This is most likely due to the support of effector functions via combined IL-7 and IL-21 cytokine signals delivered through HCW9206, which have previously been shown to synergistically promote the expansion of cytotoxic T cells and the production of inflammatory cytokines (48). However, a potential challenge of the substantial increase in inflammatory cytokine production by duoCAR THCW9206 cells compared to duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells is the risk of inducing inflammatory cytokine release. Therefore, an important future direction of this study is the further evaluation of cytokine release syndrome in our humanized mouse models. An advantage of generating CAR T cells using HCW9206 treatment compared to combinations of these or other cytokines is that it uses a single biologic to leverage the independent and synergistic effects of three cytokines, IL-7, IL-15SA, and IL-21, simplifying dosing and regulatory approval for translation into clinical use. Together, these results indicate that CAR T cell production using HCW9206 synergizes the effects of IL-7, IL-15SA, and IL-21 to promote the generation of a CAR T cell product with a diverse mix of T cell subsets, which exhibit a combination of TSCM cell self-renewal capacity and enhanced T cell effector function, likely from the TEM cell population, indicating that CAR THCW9206 cells may be a more effective and long-lasting CAR T cell immunotherapy than conventional CAR TαCD3/28 cells. RNA sequencing analysis of the transcriptome of HIV-specific duoCAR T cells generated by IL-7, IL- 15SA, and IL-21 treatment delivered by HCW9206, compared to αCD3/28 activation, identified multiple uniquely expressed genes, including those associated with increased migratory and prosurvival pathways (Fig. 5) (25, 58). Antigen stimulation of duoCAR THCW9206 cells significantly up-regulated the expression of SATB1 (special AT-rich sequence-binding protein 1), a gene previously reported to be a key determinant in lineage commitment through chromatin reorganization. Specifically, SATB1 has been shown to be a key regulator of CD8+ T cell quiescence and stemness (59–61), as well as promoting early effector cell expansion and differentiation to support both effector responses and long-term T cell persistence (61). The transcriptome of antigen-stimulated duoCAR THCW9206 cells compared to duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells also displayed increased expression of IL- 10, IL-6, and STAT3, which have been previously reported to support CAR TSCM cell maintenance and are associated with CAR T cell longevity in patients (24, 31). IPA analysis of the transcriptomes of resting duoCAR THCW9206 cells compared to resting duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells identified enrichment in DEGs associated with pathways including cell viability and cell survival (Fig. 5E). The enrichment of

genes involved in these pathways indicates the markedly increased population of long-lived, selfrenewing early memory T cell populations in the CAR THCW9206 cell product compared to the CAR TαCD3/28 cell product. Consistent with our in vitro studies, we also found that the transcriptome of antigen-stimulated duoCAR THCW9206 cells showed enrichment in gene expression in pathways associated with cytotoxicity and internalization of cells and “migratory pathways” compared to the transcriptome of antigen-stimulated duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells (fig. S9C). These results suggest that duoCAR THCW9206 cells are enriched in both a TSCM cell phenotype, as indicated by prosurvival and TSCM-like gene signatures, and more differentiated effector-like phenotypes, likely contributing to the potent HIV suppression observed both in vitro and in vivo. In a humanized mouse model of HIV infection, we demonstrated that while both intravenously injected CAR T cell types could localize to the site of HIV infection in the mouse spleen at comparable levels, anti-HIV duoCAR THCW9206 cells demonstrated superior suppression of viremia compared to duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells (Fig. 7). Specifically, we demonstrated that in the mouse spleens injected with HIV-infected human PBMCs, the duoCAR THCW9206 (92.4%) cells showed significantly increased suppression (P = 0.0173) of HIV infection compared to duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells (49.7%). This indicates that, following migration to the spleen, the duoCAR THCW9206 cells exhibited superior antiHIV cytotoxic activity compared to duoCAR TαCD3/28 cells. This enhanced activity may be a consequence of the increased population of TSCM cells in the engrafted duoCAR THCW9206 cells, which may act as a renewable source of potent effector cells to better control HIV infection. However, this study does not assess long-term anti-HIV duoCAR T efficacy against reactivated HIV-infected cells from latent HIV reservoirs. Therefore, pairing our anti-HIV duoCAR THCW9206 cells with a latencyreversing agent (LRA) may be critical to achieving a functional cure for HIV. Previous studies have shown that an IL-15 superagonist such as N-803, with a similar structure to the IL-15SA component of the HCW9206 construct, can function independently as an LRA and display both latency reversal and successful effector cell recruitment in vivo (62–66). Thus, an important future direction of this project is to evaluate HCW9206 as an LRA to be paired with treatment with anti-HIV duoCAR THCW9206 cells. Using an NSG-humanized mouse model, we observed that CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells, but not CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells, mounted a recall response to a second CD19+ NALM-6 leukemia challenge, effectively preventing tumor dissemination. This response was associated with a corresponding marked increase in CD19 CAR T cell expansion in the blood, spleen, and bone marrow of CD19 CAR THCW9206 cell–treated mice compared to those treated with CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells (Fig. 6). This correlated with the maintenance of circulating CD19 CAR TSCM cells we observed 44 days after the mice were injected with CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells but not with CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells (Fig. 6, E and F). TSCM cell enrichment in CAR T cells stimulated with HCW9206 represents a alternative strategy for generating CAR T cells, potentially providing treated patients with more prolonged and effective protection against cancer relapses.

Together, these data demonstrate that treating T cells with a combination of IL-7, IL-15SA, and IL-21 cytokines delivered by a single biologic, HCW9206, can quickly and effectively produce HIVand CD19-specific CAR T cells that are highly enriched for the TSCM cell memory phenotype, as well as effector T cells capable of maintaining suppression of HIV and leukemic cell proliferation. Therefore, generating CAR T cells through HCW9206 treatment could provide a new, improved, and highly scalable method for generating CAR T cells to treat patients with cancer and infectious diseases and replace standard CAR T cell production using αCD3/28 activation. This has widespread implications for the generation of more robust CAR T cell–based immunotherapies to improve CAR T cell functional persistence and efficacy for the treatment of HIV and cancer. MATERIALS AND METHODS Experimental design This study aims to characterize an alternative method for T cell activation that enables CAR lentiviral transduction, generating a longer-lasting, early memory TSCM cell–enriched CAR T cell phenotype with increased potency and longevity. We hypothesized that stimulating T cells with the cytokine scaffold HCW9206, which links IL-7, an IL-15SA, and IL-21, would generate CD19- and HIV-specific CAR T cells with an enriched TSCM CAR T cell memory phenotype, thereby increasing their sustained capacity to eliminate CD19+ tumors and HIV-infected cells. In our study, we evaluated HCW9206- generated CD19- and HIV-specific CAR T cells for their memory phenotype, cytotoxic function, and cytokine production. We also conducted comprehensive analyses to assess their stemlike properties, including proliferation and progeny generation assays. In addition, we conducted an in vitro HIV-1 challenge using Renilla luciferase–expressing replication-competent IMCs of HIV-1 that express different HIV-1 clade B–derived Env genes (BaL-LucR and JRCSF-LucR) to compare HIV-1 suppression between anti-HIV duoCAR TαCD3/28 and duoCAR THCW9206 cells. The in vitro studies were conducted in at least three independent donors, who were either PLWoH or PLWH. The persistence of CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells was further interrogated in a long-term in vivo NSG mouse model of CD19+ acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL). NSG mice were challenged with CD19+ firefly luciferase–expressing NALM-6–Luc cells 1 day before treatment with CD19 CAR T cells and rechallenged with NALM-6–Luc tumor 21 days into the study to evaluate the persistence of CD19 CAR THCW9206 cells compared to CD19 CAR TαCD3/28 cells. The study tracked tumor burden using IVIS bioluminescent imaging and circulating CAR T cell levels through regular peripheral blood sampling. At the end of the study, the mice were euthanized, and their spleens and bone marrow were harvested for flow cytometric analysis of CAR T cell populations. We also investigated the in vivo efficacy of anti-HIV duoCAR THCW9206 cells using hu-spl-PBMC-NSG wherein HIV-infected PBMCs are intrasplenically engrafted into the mice (hu-spl-PBMC-NSG) before intravenous duoCAR T cell delivery. At the study end point, the mice were euthanized for the following end point analysis: (i) flow cytometry of processed murine spleen for CD4+ T cell depletion, (ii) detection of duoCAR T cells by analyzing total

splenocyte-associated duoCAR DNA copies by droplet digital polymerase chain reaction (ddPCR) of mouse spleens, and (iii) HIV-1 suppression by analyzing total splenocyte-associated HIV-1 DNA by ddPCR. In all in vivo studies, at least four mice per treatment cohort were selected for statistical power, with an equal distribution of male and female mice. Mice For all in vivo experiments, >3-month-old female and male NOD.Cg-Prkdcscid RhRHIL-2rgtm1Wjl/SzJ (NSG) mice were used. NSG mice were obtained from the Jackson Laboratory (RRID: IMSR_JAX:005557) and bred at Albert Einstein College of Medicine, where they were housed in a BSL-2+ animal facility under a 12-hour light/dark cycle. Cell lines Human NALM-6 (ALL) and Raji cells (B cell Burkitt’s lymphoma) were obtained from X. Zang (Albert Einstein College of Medicine, Bronx, NY). HEK-293T cells (human kidney epithelium) and TCRβ−/− Jurkat/MA (JurMA) cells, which are a derivative of the T cell leukemia CD8+ Jurkat J.RT3-T3.5 cells expressing an NFAT promoter, were obtained as a gift from A. Follenzi and E. Hooijberg (VU University Medical Center, Amsterdam, The Netherlands), respectively (67). Cell lines were grown in Iscove’s Modified Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (IMDM) (Corning, reference no. 10-016-CV) or RPMI 1640 medium (Corning, reference no. 10-040-CV) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1× GlutaMAX (Gibco, reference no. 35050-061), 1× Penicillin-Streptomycin Solution (Corning, reference no. 30-002-CI), and 100 mM Hepes buffer and cultured in a humidified incubator at 37°C and 5% CO2. Generation of firefly luciferase+ NALM-6 cell line (NALM-6–Luc) HEK-293T cells were cotransfected with a Luciferase-tdTomato (-Luc) plasmid obtained from X. Zang (Albert Einstein College of Medicine) and three packaging plasmids [pCMV-VSV-G, pMDL (gag/pol), and REV] through calcium phosphate precipitation. Two days later, the lentiviral supernatant was concentrated by ultracentrifugation at ×20,000g for 2 hours and 15 min at 20°C. NALM-6 cells were seeded at 2 × 105 cells/ml in a six-well plate and transduced with the Luc-encoding lentivirus in the presence of LentiBlast Premium Transduction Enhancer (2 μl/ml) (OZ Biosciences, reference no. LBPX500). Transduced cells were sorted for tdTomato (red fluorescent protein) expression three times to ensure a pure NALM-6–Luc population using a BD FACSAria II cell sorter. Construction and production of HCW9206 heterodimeric fusion protein

As previously described, the heterodimeric protein scaffold, HCW9206, developed and produced by HCW Biologics, is a complex of two fusion proteins linked by a human TF scaffold (32), IL-7 linked to the N terminus of the extracellular domain (amino acids 1 to 219) of the TF and IL-21 linked to the N terminus of the sushi domain of IL-15Rα (rhIL-15RαSu). Briefly, after CHO.K1 cells [American Type Culture Collection (ATCC), reference no. CCL-61] are transfected with the two different pMSGV-1– derived vectors, they express encoded fusion proteins, which form the HCW9206 protein complex through high-affinity interactions between the rhIL-15 and rhIL-15RaSu domains. HCW9206 was purified using immunoaffinity chromatography, and the bioactivity of each cytokine domain was assessed using proliferation assays on IL-7– (2B8) (Sigma-Aldrich), IL-15– (CTLL-2) (ATCC, reference no. TIB-214), and IL-21– (B9) (Sigma-Aldrich, reference no. 12121201) dependent cell lines (32). Construction of CAR lentiviral vectors The anti-CD19 scFv (X. Zang; Albert Einstein College of Medicine, Bronx, NY) is linked to a human CD8α hinge and transmembrane domain, costimulatory molecules (CD28 and 4-1BB), and lastly the intracellular domain of human CD3ζ. The entire anti-CD19 CAR sequence was subcloned into a lentiviral expression plasmid under the control of an SFFV promoter, followed by a self-cleaving T2A peptide sequence and an enhanced green fluorescent protein (eGFP) reporter sequence for CAR detection. The lentivirus expression plasmid was modified from the pCCLsin.PPT.SFFV.IRES.eGFP.Wpre plasmid obtained from A. Follenzi (68). The anti-HIV duoCAR expression vector (MND-∆W) was generated as previously described (17). Briefly, the bicistronic antiHIV duoCAR-encoding sequence (MND-∆W) was generated wherein encoding sequences for each CAR domain (mD1.22 and m36.4) was subcloned in frame to encoding sequences for a CD8 ectodomain, CD8 transmembrane domain, 4-1BB costimulatory domain, and CD3ζ T cell signaling domain. A P2A site was also placed downstream of the first CAR (mD1.22), followed by the second CAR domain (m36.4) with a corresponding CD3ζ signaling domain. Lentiviral vector production of anti-HIV duoCAR and CD19 CARs Lentivirus expressing either anti-HIV duoCAR or CD19 CAR transgenes was produced by transiently transfecting HEK-293T cells. Using a three-plasmid system, HEK-293T cells were cotransfected with the CAR transfer plasmid (10 μg), VSVG envelope (pMD2.g; 2.5 μg), and a packaging plasmid coexpressing gag, pol, and rev (psPAX; 7.5 μg). psPAX and pMD2.g plasmids were obtained from C. Zhang (UT Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, TX). Transfection was performed in >80% confluent HEK-293T cells using jetPRIME (Polyplus, reference no. 101000046) reagents following the manufacturer’s instructions. After 2 days, the lentiviral particles were harvested from the supernatant by ultracentrifugation at 20,000g for 2 hours and 15 min at 20°C. The pellet containing the lentiviral particles was resuspended, aliquoted, and stored at −80°C. The infectious titer was calculated by

limiting dilution titration in a human JurMA cell line, wherein the lentiviral titer is expressed as transduction units per milliliter. Generation of CD19- and anti-HIV CAR T cells through lentiviral transduction PBMCs were obtained from donors recruited and leukapheresed through the Einstein-RockefellerCUNY Center for AIDS Research, which includes PLWH and PLWoH. Highly purified CD4+ and CD8+ T cells were isolated from PBMCs as previously described (16) by immunomagnetic sorting using human anti-CD4 (Miltenyi Biotec, reference no. 130-045-101) and anti-CD8 (Miltenyi Biotec, reference no. 130-045-201) microbeads according to the manufacturer’s protocol. T cells were then activated with 1 nM recombinant human IL-7 [National Institutes of Health (NIH)], IL-15 (NIH), or IL-21 (R&D Systems, catalog no. 219-IL-005) cytokines or 100 nM HCW9206 in supplemented IMDM for 7 days before lentiviral transduction. Alternatively, T cells were activated with αCD3 (OKT3) (BioLegend, reference no. 317347) and αCD28 (BioLegend, reference no. 302943) antibodies at 100 ng/ml and 2 μg/ml, respectively, in the presence of IL-2 (100 U/ml; NCI-Frederick BRB Repository, NIH) for 3 days. The IMDM was supplemented with 10% FBS, 1× GlutaMAX, 1× PenicillinStreptomycin Solution, and 100 mM Hepes buffer. After activation, T cells were transduced with αCD19-CAR or αHIV-duoCAR lentivirus in the presence of LentiBlast, as per the manufacturer’s instructions. T cells were transduced at a multiplicity of infection of 40 to 50 and spinfected at 2500 rpm (×1139g) for 60 min, and the cell culture density was adjusted to a final concentration of 5 × 105 cells/ml. Lentiviral transduction efficiency was evaluated by flow cytometric staining 5 to 10 days posttransduction, as described below. Flow cytometry of CAR T cells Approximately 5 to 10 × 105 cells untransduced or CAR-transduced primary T cells were washed with fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) buffer [1× phosphate-buffered saline with added 5% FBS and sodium azide (1 mg/ml)], incubated in human Fc blocking reagent (Miltenyi Biotec, reference no. 130-059-901) and/or mouse Fc blocking reagent (BD Biosciences, reference no. 553131) for 10 min at room temperature, and then were stained interchangeably with the following conjugated antibodies for 20 to 30 min at 4°C: CD3-VioBlue (Miltenyi Biotec, reference no. 130-113- 133), CD3-BUV395 (BD Bioscience, reference no. 563546), CD8-allophycocyanin (APC)/Cy7 (BioLegend, reference no. 301016), CD8-peridinin-chlorophyll protein complex (PerCP)/Cy5.5 (BioLegend, reference no. 344710), CD8–Pacific Blue (BioLegend, reference no. 301033), CD8– fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) (BioLegend, reference no. 344703), CD4–phycoerythrin (PE) (Miltenyi Biotec, reference no. 130-113-214), CD4-PE/Cy7 (BioLegend, reference no. 300512), CD4- FITC (BioLegend, reference no. 357405), CCR7-FITC (BioLegend, reference no. 353215), CCR7- BV711 (BioLegend, reference no. 353228), CCR7-APC (BioLegend, reference no. 353214), CD62LBV650 (BioLegend, reference no. 304832), CD27-PE/Dazzle594 (BioLegend, reference no. 356422),

CD95-BV510 (BioLegend, reference no. 305640), CD45RA-PE/Cy7 (BioLegend, reference no. 304125), CD127-PerCP/Cy5.5 (BioLegend, reference no. 351321), CD45RO-APC/Cy7 (BioLegend, reference no. 304228), CD45RO-PE (BioLegend, reference no. 304206), NKG2A-PE (BioLegend, reference no. 375103), LAG3-PE (BioLegend, reference no. 369306), LAG3-PE/Cy7 (BioLegend, reference no. 369310), PD1-FITC (Invitrogen, reference no. 11-9969-42), PD1–Alexa Fluor 700 (BioLegend, reference no. 329952), or TIM3-BV650 (BioLegend, reference no. 345027). In addition, for the detection of anti-HIV duoCAR, we used conjugated antibodies to an m36.4 idiotype, m36.4- FITC, and/or m36.4-APC (Caring Cross, Gaithersburg, MD). The GFP reporter gene was used to detect transduction with the CD19 CAR-lentivirus. In some experiments, stained cells were fixed using 2% paraformaldehyde solution before analysis. All samples were analyzed using a Cytek Aurora flow cytometer, and data analysis was performed using the FlowJo software. Cell counts are calculated on the basis of flow cytometric gating using the FlowJo software and accounting for the total staining well volume of 200 μl per sample. Intracellular cytokine secretion evaluation Donor-derived CD19-specific or anti-HIV duoCAR T cells were plated in IMDM at 2 × 106 cells/ml and cultured with target cells expressing cognate antigen, either CD19+ NALM-6 cells or HEK-293T– gp120 cells (Caring Cross, Gaithersburg, MD), respectively, at a 1:1 effector:target ratio for 16 hours in the presence of monensin (BioLegend, reference no. 420701) and brefeldin A (BioLegend, reference no. 420601) according to the manufacturer’s instructions, to prevent secretion of intracellular cytokines. Each CAR T cell analysis was set up in triplicate, and at the end of the time point, the cells were collected, washed with FACS buffer, and stained with LIVE/DEAD Fixable Viability Dye (Thermo Fisher Scientific, reference no. L34962) before incubation with human Fc block. Cells were incubated with surface staining antibodies for 30 min at 4°C: CD8-APC/Cy7, CD45RO-PE, CCR7-APC, and m36.4-FITC (or GFP for CD19 CAR). Following incubation, cells were fixed using 2% paraformaldehyde and permeabilized using eBioscience Foxp3/Transcription Factor Staining Buffer Set (eBioscience, reference no. 00-5523-00) according to the manufacturer’s protocol. Subsequently, the cells were incubated with the following intracellular antibodies for 1 hour at room temperature or at 4°C overnight: IL-2-PerCP/Cy5.5 (BioLegend, reference no. 500321), IFN-γ–BV605 (BioLegend, reference no. 502536), TNFα-PE/Cy7 (BD Biosciences, reference no. 560678), TNFα- BV711 (BioLegend, reference no. 502939), or TCF1–Alexa Fluor 647 (BioLegend, reference no. 655203). T cell proliferation and STAT5/STAT3 phosphorylation For T cell proliferation assays, PBMCs derived from PLWoH were thawed and stained with CTV (2 μM) (Invitrogen, reference no. C34557) for 20 min at 37°C. PBMCs were then stimulated with either anti-CD3/anti-CD28 in the presence of IL-2 (100 U/ml) or HCW9206 (100 nM). Seven days after

stimulation, cells were stained for the level of CTV expression and analyzed by flow cytometry. Similarly, to assess STAT5 or STAT3 phosphorylation, PBMCs were thawed, and CD8 T cells were isolated as described above. Isolated T cells were plated overnight in a flat 96-well plate (2 × 106 cells/ml) in serum-free IMDM. Plated cells were either unstimulated or stimulated with 1 nM IL-7, IL- 15, IL-21, or 100 nM HCW9206 and rested for 15 to 30 min at 37°C. Paraformaldehyde solution (1.6%) was added to each well and incubated at room temperature for 10 min. Cells were washed with FACS buffer and centrifuged at 1300 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was removed, and chilled methanol (100 μl) was added to each well to permeabilize the cells for 30 min at 4°C. The cells were then washed with FACS buffer and resuspended in phospho-STAT 5 (pSTAT5)–Alexa Fluor 647 (BioLegend, reference no. 612599) or pSTAT3-FITC antibody (BioLegend, reference no. 651020). Cells were washed with FACS buffer before flow cytometric analysis. In vitro anti-HIV duoCAR T cell killing of HIV-1–infected donor cells CD4+ T cells derived from HIV seronegative donors or PBMCs derived from HIV seropositive donors were cultured in supplemented IMDM and activated with IL-2 (100 U/ml) and PHA-L (4 μg/ml) (Roche, reference no. 11249738001) for 72 hours before infection with HIV-LucR-IMC as previously described (16, 17). For studies using PLWH donors, CD8+ T cells were depleted from donors’ PBMCs through immunomagnetic CD8 bead isolation before activation. Autologous activated CD4 T cells or CD8-depleted PBMCs were washed, plated in a 96-well U-bottom plate with 5 × 104 or 10 × 105 cells per well, respectively, and spinfected at 2500 rpm (×1139g) for 90 min at room temperature with the indicated HIV-LucR IMC virus (~1 × 106 IU/ml). The HIV-LucR IMC used in this study were VRC01-sensitive HIV-1 Env strains JRCSF and BaL (69). As previously described, the virus stocks were titered (in international units per milliliter) on the TZM-bl reporter cell line [NIH AIDS Research and Reference Reagent Program, Division of AIDS, National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID), NIH, reference no. 8219] (70). Infection was established for 3 days before coculture with autologous CD8+ anti-HIV duoCAR T cells or UTD control T cells at the indicated effector:target ratios. The cocultures were incubated for an additional 3 days, after which the cells were lysed, and HIV-1 infection was quantified by measuring relative luciferase (LucR) using the Renilla Luciferase System (Promega, reference no. E2820) as previously described (70). The percentage of HIV-1 suppression was calculated as (1 – CARRLU/HIV+RLU or UTDRLU) × 100%. Europium-TDA in vitro CD19 CAR T cell killing assay Cytotoxic activity of CD19-specific CAR T cells was assessed using DELFIA Europium-TDA (EuTDA) Cytotoxicity Reagents (PerkinElmer, reference no. AD0116) and CD19+ NALM-6 or Raji cells as target cells. Target cells were stained with BATDA reagent (1.3 μl/1 × 106 cells) and incubated for 30 min at 37°C. Target cells were washed with a medium containing probenecid (2.5 mM) to reduce the background signal. Target cells (1 × 105) were plated in a 96-well U-bottom plate and cocultured

with CD19 CAR T cells or UTD control T cells for 3 hours at 37°C at the indicated effector:target ratios. After incubation, supernatant (20 μl) was transferred to a microtiter plate, and Europium Solution (200 μl per well) was added. After 15 min of incubation on a plate shaker at room temperature, time-resolved fluorescence was quantified on a fluorescent plate reader equipped with DELFIA time-resolved fluorescence detection. The percentage of target cell–specific lysis was calculated using the formula: % Specific lysis = (experimental release – spontaneous release)/(maximum release – spontaneous release) × 100%. Progeny generation assays and inhibitory receptor assays To assay CAR T cell progeny generation in TSCM cell–enriched CAR THCW9206 cells, CAR T cells were cocultured with target cells expressing cognate antigen for 4 days. CD19-specific or HIV-specific duoCAR T cells were phenotyped for memory markers using FACS analysis before coculturing with NALM-6 or 293T-gp120 target cells, respectively, at a 2:1 effector:target ratio for 4 days on a 24-well plate. After coculture, cells were collected and phenotyped at the end time point using flow cytometric analysis. Similarly, the expression of inhibitory receptors was assayed in CAR T cells either before or after 24 hours of coculture with cognate antigen. Specifically, 293T-gp120 target cells (2 × 104) were plated in a 96-well U-bottom plate and cocultured with HIV-specific duoCAR T cells (1 × 105), respectively (5:1 effector:target). One day after coculture, the cells were collected and stained for CAR expression and inhibitory receptor expression (e.g., PD-1, TIM3, LAG3) and analyzed by flow cytometry. RNA sequencing: Library preparation CD8+ CAR T cells generated following HCW9206 (100 nM) or αCD3/28 activation with added IL-2 (100 U/ml) were cocultured for 16 hours with HEK-293T (293T) or 293T-gp120 cells at a 5:1 effector:target ratio in supplemented IMDM. After 16 hours, cells were collected, washed with FACS buffer, and stained with αCD8-APC/Cy7 and the duoCAR detection antibody (m36.4-FITC). In addition, cells were resuspended in a viability dye (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) (Sigma-Aldrich) before sorting the CD8+ m36.4+ duoCAR T cells into RLT Plus lysis buffer using a BD FACSAria II cell sorter. RNA was purified using the micro-RNeasy Plus kit following the manufacturer’s instructions (QIAGEN, reference no. 74034). This protocol was repeated for each biological replicate. RNA sequencing was performed using Novogene’s human mRNA sequencing pipeline, including RNA sample quality control (QC), RNA purification, mRNA library preparation (polyadenylate enrichment), and the NovaSeq X Plus Series (PE150) sequencing platform. RNA sequencing: Read processing, alignment, and quantification

Quality control assessments of raw sequencing reads were performed using FastQC (version 0.11.9, Babraham Bioinformatics), validating data quality. Adapter sequences and low-quality bases were trimmed using Cutadapt (version 2.0) to ensure high-quality read retention. The processed reads were then mapped to the GRCh38human genome reference using the STAR aligner (version 2.4.2a) with default parameters. Following alignment, gene-level read quantification was conducted using featureCounts (version 1.5.0, from the Subread package), using gene annotations from the ENSEMBL GTF file (release 110). RNA sequencing: Differential expression analysis Gene expression differences were analyzed using DESeq2 (version 1.34.0) in R. To minimize noise from low-expressed genes, filtering was applied to remove genes where at least 80% of samples had fewer than 10 raw counts or where all samples exhibited zero expression. DESeq2 uses the Wald test to assess statistical significance, with multiple testing corrections performed via the BenjaminiHochberg procedure. Genes with an padj < 0.05 and an absolute log2 fold change >1.5 were classified as significantly differentially expressed. Regularized log-transformed (rlogTransformation) count data were used for principal components analysis and hierarchical clustering–based heatmaps, allowing visualization of global expression patterns across samples. For gene pathway analysis, IPA (QIAGEN) was used. DEGs for each comparison were uploaded for analysis, with filtering for observed relationships across all species and cell lines, excluding categories containing the terms “cancer” or “injury.” The top predicted gene functions were identified as functions with the lowest P values, followed by the highest absolute value associated with z-score, with parameters of padj ≤ 0.05, |log2 (fold change)| ≥ 1, and |z-score| ≥ 2. For the preranked GSEA, 289 DEGs (padj < 0.05) were ranked from the most significantly up-regulated to the most significantly downregulated genes. Analysis of Hallmark gene sets was performed at 1000 permutations, a minimum gene set size of 10 (filtering out 40 of 50 gene sets), and classic enrichment statistics. In vivo intravenous NALM-6 leukemia tumor NSG mouse model NSG mice were intravenously injected with NALM-6–Luc cells (5 × 105 cells) 1 day before engraftment with CD19 CAR T cells or UTD control T cells at an 8:1 effector:target ratio and a 1:1 ratio of CD4:CD8 effector T cells. Tumor-bearing mice were rechallenged with intravenously infused NALM-6–Luc cells (5 × 105 cells) 21 days after the initial tumor injection. All mice were euthanized at a single end point (day 44). Tumor burden was tracked via IVIS bioluminescent imaging over 44 days. At the end point, mice were euthanized, and peripheral blood, spleen, and bone marrow were harvested for immune cell isolation and flow cytometric analysis. In vivo hu-spl-PBMC-NSG mouse model of HIV-1 infection

We studied the in vivo efficacy of anti-HIV duoCAR T cells using our previously described hu-splPBMC-NSG mouse model (16, 17). NSG mice were intrasplenically injected with HIV-infected PBMCs (15 × 106 cells), and then duoCAR T cells were intravenously delivered at a 1:1 effector:target ratio, along with a 1:1 ratio of CD4:CD8 effector T cells. Mice were euthanized at a single end point (days 17 to 18). Spleens were harvested at the end point, and immune cells/splenocytes were isolated for further analyses, including quantification of total cell-associated HIV-DNA and duoCARDNA in total splenocytes by ddPCR, and T cell analysis by flow cytometry. Bioluminescent imaging All studies using bioluminescent imaging were performed using the IVIS Spectrum in vivo Imaging System (PerkinElmer), and data analysis was completed using the Living Image software (version 3.0). All mice received an intraperitoneal injection of D-luciferin [mouse (150 μg/g)] (Gold Biotechnology, reference no. LUCNA) and were scanned at 15 min postinjection. Immune cell isolation from mouse blood, spleen, and bone marrow Bone marrow cells were harvested from mouse femurs, filtered through a 70 μM strainer, washed, and centrifuged at 3500 rpm (×1595g) for 10 min. The supernatant was removed, and cells were incubated with 1× red blood cell (RBC) lysis buffer for 1.5 min at 4°C. In parallel, spleens were harvested from mice, disassociated, filtered through a 70 μM strainer, washed, and centrifuged at 1300 rpm (×592g) for 10 min. Cells were resuspended in 1× RBC lysis buffer and incubated for 5 min at 4°C. Peripheral blood (200 μl per mouse) was collected from submandibular bleeding into anticoagulant EDTA-coated collection tubes. Blood was strained through a 70 μM strainer, washed, and resuspended in 1× RBC lysis buffer. All cells were washed and resuspended in FACS buffer before flow cytometric staining. Human T cell counts from blood, bone marrow, and spleen were quantified via flow cytometric analysis using the FlowJo software, with absolute cell numbers normalized to the total processed sample volume. Plasma was isolated from peripheral blood via centrifugation of EDTA-coated collection tubes at 8000 rpm (×3645g) for 8 min. Detection of total cell-associated HIV DNA in mouse splenocytes by ddPCR Total HIV DNA in the spleens of hu-spl-PBMC-NSG mice was detected by ddPCR using primers that target a region with the HIV-1 Gag gene that is not present in the CAR lentivirus itself, after following the manufacturer’s instructions. DNA was isolated from whole spleens using the AllPrep DNA/RNA Kit (QIAGEN, reference no. 80204). The DNA concentration was determined using a NanoDrop 2000 Spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific), and the copies of HIV-1 Gag DNA (25 ng/μl) in the mouse spleens were quantified using PrimePCR Custom Assay HIV primer pairs/probe mixes (Bio-

Rad, Hercules, California). Specifically, we used the HIV-Gag primer/probe mix sequences of the HIVGag3 region: forward primer (5′-TCAGCCCAGAAGTAATACCCATGT-3′), reverse primer (5′- CACTGTGTTTAGCATGGTGTTT-3′), and probe (5′-ATTATCAGAAGGAGCCACCCCACAAGA-3′) with dye quencher (5′ 6-FAM, 3’ Iowa Black FQ). A splenic DNA sample (250 ng) was amplified using the primers/probes specific to HIV-1 Gag, and each run included an HIV-negative human sample and a nontemplate control. ddPCR HIV-1 Gag sample results were quantified by the QuantaSoft software and reported as copies per microliter of DNA for each sample. All samples were then normalized to 25 ng/μl of DNA. Results were displayed as total HIV-1 Gag copies/1 × 106 splenocytes. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) between groups using GraphPad Prism with significance set at P < 0.05. Study approval Written informed consent was obtained from PBMC donors under protocols approved by the Institutional Review Boards of the Albert Einstein College of Medicine and Rockefeller University (protocol #2017-8116). Animal studies were conducted under a protocol approved by the Albert Einstein College of Medicine Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (protocol #0001105) and were performed in accordance with the NIH Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. Statistical analysis All statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism (version 9.5.1), with the statistical tests described in each figure legend. For all figures except Fig. 5, which represents the RNA sequencing dataset, data are presented as mean ± SEM from triplicate technical replicates, with statistical tests performed across biological replicates for a minimum of N = 3 donors. For RNA sequencing analyses, the data represent biological replicates from two independent donors.
